**April 16, 2026 Holland Charter Township Board of Trustees Meeting**

- **Public Comment on Law Enforcement Disparity**: Resident Steve Willard presented data showing Holland Township has the highest exhaust complaint volume in Ottawa County but receives the least enforcement by the Sheriff's Office (4 citations vs. Georgetown's 17 in 12 months). Board was asked to formally raise the contract performance issue with the Sheriff's Office and request regular reporting.

- **Legal Counsel Update on Lawn Avenue Dispute**: Attorney Ron Boli reviewed the long-running DeYoung property dispute, confirming the 2020 mediated settlement resolved all claims with prejudice. He explicitly stated Trustee DeYoung has no further legal claim, and denied DeYoung's recent public statements that Boli advised vacating Lawn Avenue. Boli also recommended proper chain-of-command procedures for employee complaints and warned against Open Meetings Act violations via email exchanges among board members.

- **Zoning Ordinance Amendment Approved**: Second reading of rezoning on Quincy Street (property behind Blake Care) from EG to residential passed unanimously (7-0).

- **Public Safety Millage Renewal**: Resolution to place 1.75-mill public safety levy on August 4 primary ballot passed 7-0. The levy (covering 2027–2030) funds fire department primary medical response and maintains sheriff staffing. Projects $5M+ in costs; general fund contribution expected to stabilize around current $2M level by 2031.

- **Water Main Replacement Project**: Major infrastructure work on Lakewood Boulevard beginning early May will close eastbound lanes and restrict westbound traffic through mid-summer. Project extends through 120th Avenue and northward; disruption expected due to limited river crossings.

- **Trustee DeYoung Raised Concerns**: DeYoung objected that he was not notified in advance that legal counsel would appear to address the Lawn Avenue matter, limiting his preparation. He also raised ongoing concerns about selective parking enforcement (11 years unresolved), requested investigation into a prior closed-session employee complaint, and asked about revisiting the South Oak Lawn road abandonment.

- **Administrative/Community Support**: Approved contracted community services funding for American Legion Band ($3,000), Hospice of Holland, Holland Community Theater ($500), and Zealand Historical Society. Approved two quick-claim deeds for abandoned street right-of-way on Oddwas Street. Comcast franchise agreement renewal announced for next year.

Crds8gPkKoo.summary:
**March 19, 2026 Board of Trustees Meeting Summary**

- **Agenda Amendment**: Trustee Dave DeYoung moved to add Item 13—"Update on Selective Enforcement by Holland Charter Township Staff"—which passed unanimously.

- **Zoning Amendments**: Board introduced first reading of zoning text amendment (Section 914) to permit chickens and bees in residential areas (R1/R2) with conditions. Extensive discussion highlighted need for pilot program, shorter permit renewal periods, permit revocation mechanisms, and coordination with City of Holland's experience. Staff directed to provide additional details before second reading.

- **Conditional Zoning Approval**: Second reading and adoption of conditional zoning for 188 North Jefferson/West McKinley (I2/R2 to C2 community commercial) passed 6–0 on roll call.

- **Parks & Recreation Plan**: Board approved $14,500 contract with MCSA Group to update township parks and recreation plan; includes public survey and needs assessment. Trustee noted interest in improving Dutton Park waterfront.

- **Fire Department Cadet Program**: Resolution authorizing Fire Chief to establish cadet/explorer program through Careerline Tech Center passed unanimously; provides recruitment pipeline for 16–17-year-olds interested in fire/EMS careers.

- **Mobile Oxygen Generator**: Fire department capital expenditure of $83,489 for Novair USA oxygen generation system approved 5–1 (DeYoung opposed) to reduce refill costs and enable emergency mutual aid to surrounding departments.

- **Cleaning Services Contract**: After competitive bid process, board awarded $341,811 annual contract to Envirro Clean (selected over lower bidder due to superior staffing depth and quality control); includes day porter for new community center.

- **Vehicle Replacement**: Board approved $181,130 from Barber Ford for five half-ton pickups plus $10,000 lighting package; brief discussion of supporting local township businesses (Robert Denooer Chevrolet also bid).

- **Selective Enforcement Presentation & Discussion**: Citizen Steve Willard presented detailed analysis of three enforcement cases showing pattern of delayed/no action on violations (modified exhaust, unregistered vehicles, road right-of-way parking). Trustee DeYoung raised his own selective enforcement concerns, including storage shed setback requirements, road right-of-way parking enforcement at his property, and Lawn Avenue road abandonment. DeYoung called for independent third-party investigation; motion did not receive support. Board committed to obtaining promised update on 610 Northshore Drive enforcement within next meeting. New full-time code enforcement officer started this week; sheriff's department has reorganized with new lieutenant focused on improvement.

**Board of Trustees Meeting – Holland Charter Township, February 5, 2026**

- **Public Comment on Code Enforcement:** Citizen presented detailed 24-page analysis documenting systemic enforcement gaps at 11813 Greenley Street over five months, requesting formal performance review of Community Development Director Cory Brosma with consideration for termination due to incomplete enforcement and ignored citizen evidence.

- **Road Commission Projects:** County road commission presented summer projects including Butternut Drive resurfacing, Baseline/Greenland road improvements, 120th Street work with proposed roundabout at Port Sheldon, and 104th Bridge rebuild. Discussed pedestrian safety concerns and farmer concerns about roundabout impacts.

- **Tax Abatements Approved:** NextGen Services LLC approved for 12-year IFC exemption ($2.44M capital investment, 8 new jobs); VoltageGen LLC approved for IFC transfer (15 current employees, median wage ~$130,000/year, new profit-share program implemented). Dave DeYoung questioned wage commitments and whether tax savings would be shared with employees.

- **MRC Update & Budget:** Mackinaw Resource Center director Leroy presented annual update showing 3,000+ people served; board increased MRC contract from $56,000 to $76,000 to support mortgage paydown and capital campaign, pending establishment of improved community board structure.

- **Brownfield Authority:** Warren Smith reappointed; intergovernmental agreement approved for Ottawa County Brownfield program management services.

- **Code Enforcement Concerns (Extended Discussion):** Steve presented 2025 enforcement report (1,277 complaints, 1.55 FTE staff, ~20 citations issued). Board discussed understaffing (moving to 2 full-time positions), proactive vs. reactive enforcement, rental unit inspections (2,600 units on 3-year cycle), and need for aging report on unresolved complaints over 90 days.

- **Crane Truck Purchase:** Michigan Kenworth approved as low bid at $220,291 (roll call vote: 6–0–1).

- **Dave DeYoung's Lawn Avenue Road Abandonment Update:** Extensive personal statement detailing frustration with staff's resistance to final property deed transfer. DeYoung reported meeting with Chairman Tesla regarding new real estate attorney's claim that "easements do not run with the land," which DeYoung disputes. - **Housing Analysis Presentation**: McKenna Associates presented a comprehensive housing demand and supply analysis for Holland Charter Township (2025). Key findings: township projected to need 3,000-6,000 new residents by 2040 (42,000-45,000 total population); current housing supply of ~15,700 units adequate for near-term demand; comprehensive plan allows ~10,000 additional units if fully built out, providing sufficient capacity through 2040; recommendation to NOT adjust future land use map or add more housing density. - **Planning Commission Input**: Randy Cordering (Planning Commission Chair) presented that commission reviewed report three times and approved it. - **Other Business**: Approved election equipment maintenance agreement with county (annual standard practice, shared cost); reminded trustees of September 4 budget study session at 5 p.m. with dinner; noted major soccer tournament event (300+ teams) coming to township parks this weekend.

**Holland Charter Township Planning Commission Meeting – November 4, 2025**

- **Comprehensive Housing Study**: Commission voted unanimously (6-0) to recommend approval of the updated comprehensive plan incorporating a new housing analysis chapter. The study confirms the township's current plan is adequate to meet projected housing demand through 2040 (42,000–45,000 residents), provided the township maintains approximately 250 housing units per year of construction. Staff noted the need for ongoing dialogue with developers about housing types (e.g., single-family homes vs. rentals) and potential zoning adjustments to encourage ownership housing and address affordability gaps.

- **Emanuel Church Special Land Use & Expansion**: Commission approved three-phase expansion (pole barn, office/youth space addition, and picnic shelter with restrooms) with conditions: construction must begin within one year; staff to review parking layout and aesthetic details; outdoor activities/services permitted if non-disruptive to neighbors; temporary barriers required for outdoor sales areas. Fire code issue regarding Phase 3 (two-hour fire wall separation) was resolved by applicant.

- **Wetland Mitigation Banks & Front Yard Definition**: Commission unanimously recommended approval of zoning text amendments (Articles 9 and 22) to define wetland mitigation banks as a permitted use in agricultural districts and clarify front yard setback requirements (referenced to lot lines rather than adjacent buildings). EGLE (state) was consulted; language added to allow non-motorized trails and public amenities unless planning commission prohibits.

- **Lowe's Outdoor Display Expansion**: Approved outdoor sales/display of non-combustible items (mowers, tillers, grills, etc.) with explicit exclusion of side-by-side vehicles and golf carts (which would require separate vehicle sales permit). Parking and barriers to be reviewed by staff per existing standards.

- **Backyard Chickens Ordinance**: Commission recommended approval of text amendment allowing up to 6 chickens per household in R1 and R2 residential zones, with setback requirements matching accessory buildings (7 ft. from side lot lines, 5 ft. rear). Permit process details (terminology, inspection requirements, fees) left to township board/staff to finalize; no roosters permitted; indoor slaughtering only.

- **Mac Legends PUD Amendment & Other Business**: Approved PUD amendment for Phase 1 (expiration date December 31, 2033). Staff updates included upcoming Gearlings project review (bridge removal decision), Lemon Fresh building demolition funded by blight grant, and initial planning for mixed-use development on 128th Street.

• **Housing Study Presentation & Approval**: McKenna consultants presented comprehensive 2025 housing market analysis showing Holland Township is at supply-demand parity currently but faces potential shortage if migration rates increase; 70/30 ownership-to-rental split projected; demand for 3,000-5,000 new owner-occupied units and 800-1,200 rental units by 2040. dFNihAvD1eA.summary:
- **Item: Great Lakes Partners Lot Split Variance Request** – Applicant Bill Sickle presented a request for a variance to allow splitting approximately 2.47 acres (modified proposal) from an 80-acre farm parcel to sell the existing farmhouse while preserving farmland. The 5-acre minimum is required by ordinance; practical difficulty cited is a creek that meanders through the property, creating awkward configurations if complied with.
- **Key Discussion Points** – Board thoroughly debated competing interests: preserving farmland (applicant's intent) versus enforcing the 5-acre minimum zoning ordinance adopted 5 years ago. Alternative solutions raised included leasing the house, selling with a farming easement, or challenging the ordinance itself. Applicant argued the variance *preserves* farmland by keeping 77+ acres available for continuous farming rather than chunking off larger mixed-use parcels.
- **Standards Analysis** – Board reviewed all six variance standards. Consensus: (1) practical difficulty exists due to creek; (2) creek is not unique to most properties; (3) variance not strictly *necessary* (alternatives exist, though less ideal); (4) no substantial detriment to adjacent properties; (5) applicant created the problem by proposing split; (6) granting variance conflicts with ordinance's spirit of preserving low-density agricultural character.
- **Vote Results** – Motion to approve modified 2.2-acre variance (Ross De, seconded by Elliott Church) **failed 2-3** (De, Church yes; Mackey, De, Bander no). Motion to deny the request **passed 3-2** (Mackey, De, Bander yes; De, Church no). Final decision: **Variance DENIED**.
- **Notable Public Comment** – None; public hearing was closed in September.
- **Dave DeYoung Reference** – No mention of Trustee Dave DeYoung in this Zoning Board of Appeals meeting.

- **Consent Agenda Approved**: Board approved April 2026 meeting minutes, building permits ($13M+ in construction costs including Emanuel Baptist addition and Meyer renovations), fire department report (223 calls, retirement of 54-year firefighter Jim Degraph recognized), and sheriff report (1,218 calls for service).
